title: CIL System — Advanced Mock 14 (Conceptual)
pattern: CIL MT — 200 MCQ · 2 papers · 1 mark each · no negative marking

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: Government's capital expenditure outlay for FY26-27 was budgeted at:
questions.solution: ₹11.11 lakh cr

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: What does GDP at factor cost exclude?
questions.solution: Indirect taxes

questions.solution: USA, Canada, Mexico

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.stem: Fiscal deficit is:
options: Total expenditure minus total receipts excluding borrowings
questions.solution: Total expenditure minus total receipts excluding borrowings

questions.topic: Static GK · Coal & Mining in India

questions.solution: Gevra

questions.subject: Paper-I · General Awareness
questions.topic: Static GK · Coal & Mining in India
questions.stem: Proximate analysis of coal determines:
questions.solution: Moisture, Volatile, Ash, Fixed Carbon

questions.stem: A boat travels 32 km upstream in 4 h and 48 km downstream in 4 h. Stream speed:
questions.solution: 4 km/h

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: Which is not a measure of central tendency?
questions.solution: Standard deviation

questions.stem: How many numbers between 1000 and 9999 have no repeated digits?

questions.stem: A vessel has 60 L milk. 12 L removed and replaced with water. Milk in final mixture:

questions.stem: Of three numbers, the first is twice the second and thrice the third. Average of all three is 44. The largest number is:

questions.stem: If 6 men or 8 women or 12 children can do in 9 days, 3 men + 4 women + 6 children in:

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: A man takes 6 h for a 60 km journey partly by car (80 km/h) and rest by cycle (12 km/h). Distance by cycle:

questions.stem: A conical flask radius 7 cm, height 24 cm is full of water poured into a cylindrical flask radius 14 cm. Height of water:

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: Walking at ¾ of usual speed, a man is 10 min late. Usual time:
questions.solution: 30 min

questions.stem: A sum at 10% CI in 3 years becomes ₹13,310. Sum:

questions.stem: A completes a lap in 54 sec, B in 72 sec. When will they meet again at start?
questions.solution: 3 min 36 sec

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: Difference between CI and SI for 2 years at 5% on ₹10,000:

questions.stem: A can do a work in 15 days. B is 25% more efficient. B alone takes:
questions.solution: 12 days

questions.stem: If 30% of (x - y) = 15% of (x + y), then x : y is:

questions.stem: CI on ₹2,000 at 10% for 2 years, compounded annually:

questions.subject: Paper-I · Numerical Ability
questions.stem: A boat goes 24 km upstream and 28 km downstream in 8 h. 30 km upstream and 21 km downstream in 9 h. Speed of stream:
questions.solution: 4 km/h

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: From point A, B is 5 km north. C is 10 km east of B. D is 5 km south of C. How far A to D?
questions.solution: 10 km

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Mirror image of 'CALCULATOR' placed horizontally:
questions.solution: ROTALUCLAC

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: A is B's sister. C is B's mother. D is C's father. How is A related to D?
questions.solution: Granddaughter

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Between 2 and 3, when are hands at right angle?

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Statements: Some X are Y. All Y are Z. No Z is W. All W are V. Conclusions: I. Some X are not W. II. No Y is W.
questions.solution: Both follow

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Five girls sit in a row. Pooja is left of Ritu but right of Sana. Tina is right of Ritu. Uma is left of Sana. Who is in middle?
questions.solution: Pooja

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Ravi walks 4 km south, 3 km east, 4 km north. Shortest distance from start:
questions.solution: 3 km

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: A clock shows 6:20. Mirror image time:

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: X and Y are brothers. Z is the daughter of Y. W is the son of X's sister. How is W related to Z?
questions.solution: Cousin

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: What was the day on 26 January 1950?
questions.solution: Thursday

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Statements: All birds can fly. Some creatures that fly are bats. All bats are mammals. Conclusions: I. Some birds are bats. II. Some mammals are not birds.
questions.solution: Neither follows

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: In a row, Amit is 5th from left and Sumit is 6th from right. They exchange positions. Now Amit is 13th from left. Sumit's new position from right:
questions.solution: 14th

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: A walks 30 m north, turns right 40 m, right 50 m, right 40 m. Distance from start:

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: If mirror is placed to the right of the word EXAM, the image shows:
questions.solution: MAXE

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: P is the son of Q. R is the daughter of Q. S is the brother of P. How is S related to R?
questions.solution: Brother

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: At what time between 8 and 9 are hands opposite?

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: A committee has 4 members with 2 boys and 2 girls. Ravi and Simran cannot be together. In how many ways can they be selected from 5 boys and 5 girls if Ravi and Simran are among them?

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: A company spends 35% salary, 20% raw material, 15% marketing, 10% R&D, rest profit. If profit is ₹20 cr, total expenditure:
questions.solution: ₹80 cr

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: In a code, SACHIN = 58, DRAVID = 49, then GANGULY = ?

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Should there be a single national mining regulatory authority? Arg I: Yes, it ensures uniform standards. Arg II: No, states have different mining contexts.
questions.solution: Both are strong

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: In a circle of 8, M is opposite N. O is 2nd right of M. P is adjacent to N. Q is between O and M. Who is 2nd left of P?
questions.solution: O

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: A is 5 km south of B. C is 12 km east of A. D is 5 km north of C. Distance B to D:
questions.solution: 12 km

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: 7 chairs in a row. P sits 3rd from left. Q sits 2nd right of P. R sits left of Q. S sits between P and R. Who sits 2nd from right?
questions.solution: Q

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Find the missing: 1, 0, 3, 2, 5, 4, ?

questions.subject: Paper-I · Reasoning
questions.stem: Find the wrong term: 1, 3, 6, 10, 15, 21, 28, 37

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: The workers went on strike, ____ production.
questions.solution: halting

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Find the error: He is (A) / more smarter (B) / than his (C) / elder brother. (D)
questions.solution: B - should be 'smarter'

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: The new policy is ____ to improve efficiency.
questions.solution: designed

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Find the error: She prefers (A) / coffee than (B) / tea in (C) / the morning. (D)
questions.solution: B - should be 'to'

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: The organisation ____ its doors to the public.
questions.solution: opened

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Find the error: I am looking (A) / forward to (B) / meet you (C) / next week. (D)
questions.solution: C - should be 'meeting'

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: The new regulation ____ into effect next month.
questions.solution: comes

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Find the error: It is (A) / high time (B) / we leave (C) / for the station. (D)
questions.solution: C - should be 'left'

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: The mine was ____ after the accident.
questions.solution: sealed

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Find the error: The number of (A) / accidents have (B) / increased this (C) / year compared to last. (D)
questions.solution: B - should be 'has'

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: The accident ____ a review of safety protocols.
questions.solution: prompted

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Find the error: One of the miners (A) / have been (B) / hospitalised after (C) / the accident. (D)
questions.solution: B - should be 'has been'

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: The court ____ the contract void.
questions.solution: declared

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Find the error: She not only (A) / lost her wallet (B) / but also (C) / her mobile phone. (D)
options: A - should be 'She lost not only'
questions.solution: A - should be 'She lost not only'

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: The evidence ____ against the accused.
questions.solution: testified

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Find the error: The data (A) / collected from (B) / the site is (C) / being analysed. (D)
questions.solution: C - should be 'are'

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: The document ____ the terms of the agreement.
questions.solution: specifies

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: The manager asked ____ the report was ready.
questions.solution: whether

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Rearrange: solve / team / the / to / problem / together / worked
options: The team worked together to solve the problem
Worked together the team to solve the problem
Together the team worked to solve the problem
To solve the problem the team together worked
questions.solution: The team worked together to solve the problem

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Meaning of 'To give the benefit of the doubt':
questions.solution: To assume someone is honest

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Antonym of COMPLACENT:
questions.solution: Dissatisfied

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Meaning of 'To get down to brass tacks':
questions.solution: To focus on essentials

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Antonym of BENEVOLENT:
questions.solution: Cruel

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Synonym of TRANQUIL:
questions.solution: Serene

questions.subject: Paper-I · General English
questions.stem: Antonym of BELLIGERENT:
questions.solution: Peaceful

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Preemptive scheduling:
options: The OS can interrupt a running process and switch to another
questions.solution: Preemptive: timer interrupt triggers context switch. Non-preemptive: process yields voluntarily.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: An n-way set-associative cache:
options: Each set has n lines; block goes to one set, any line within
questions.solution: Set-associative: compromise between direct-mapped (conflict misses) and full (slow, expensive).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The language of balanced parentheses is:
questions.solution: Balanced parentheses = classic CFG (Dyck language).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Container_of macro in Linux kernel:
options: Creates a container for an object
Given a pointer to a struct member, returns pointer to the containing struct
questions.solution: container_of(ptr, type, member): uses offsetof to compute containing struct address.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Memory access = 100 ns, page-fault rate = 0.002, fault service = 8 ms. The effective access time is about:
questions.solution: EAT = (1−p)·ma + p·service = 1×100 + 0.002×8ms ≈ 16099.8 ns (faults dominate).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Denormalized numbers in IEEE 754:
options: Have the same precision as normalized
Fill the underflow gap (exponent = all 0s, mantissa represents subnormal values)
questions.solution: Denormals: gradual underflow. Represent values smaller than min normal. Processor may flush to zero.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A language is recursively enumerable if:
options: A TM accepts all strings in the language (may loop on others)
A TM halts on all inputs
questions.solution: RE (Type-0): TM accepts w∈L, may not halt for w∉L. Recursive = always halts.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Weak_ptr solves:
options: The circular reference problem in shared_ptr (doesn't increase ref count)
questions.solution: weak_ptr: non-owning observer. lock() returns shared_ptr if object still alive.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The kernel mode has:
options: Full access to hardware and all memory
questions.solution: Kernel mode (ring 0): privileged instructions, full memory, I/O access.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: In the Von Neumann architecture, instructions and data:
questions.solution: Von Neumann machines store programs and data in one common memory (Harvard uses separate).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A DFA is:
options: A finite automaton with exactly one transition per symbol per state
questions.solution: DFA: deterministic (δ: Q×Σ → Q). NFA: δ: Q×Σ → P(Q) (multiple choices).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Polymorphism in OOP allows:
options: One interface to take many forms
questions.solution: Polymorphism lets the same call behave differently depending on the object's type.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Page replacement algorithms include:
questions.solution: FIFO = oldest first. LRU = least recently used. Optimal = future knowledge. Clock = approximate LRU.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Cache access = 20 ns, memory access = 200 ns, hit ratio = 0.8. The effective access time is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The class NP consists of problems:
options: Verifiable in polynomial time by a deterministic Turing machine
Solvable in polynomial time on NTM
questions.solution: NP = problems verifiable in polynomial time. Also solvable in poly time on NTM.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: RAII (Resource Acquisition Is Initialization):
options: Binds resource lifetime to object lifetime (acquire in constructor, release in destructor)
questions.solution: RAII: smart pointers (unique_ptr, shared_ptr), mutex locks, file handles (exception-safe).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: cgroups (control groups):
options: Limit and isolate resource usage (CPU, memory, I/O) for process groups
Control which users can run processes
questions.solution: cgroups: used by Docker/LXC for container resource limits. cgroup v2 (unified hierarchy).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: How many address lines are needed to address 64K memory locations?
questions.solution: 64K = 65536 = 2^16, so 16 address lines are required.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The subset sum problem is:
questions.solution: Subset sum: given integers, find subset summing to target. NP-complete.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: An enum in C:
options: Defines a set of named integer constants
questions.solution: enum: values default to 0, 1, 2... Can assign custom values. Type-safe in C++.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The Banker's algorithm prevents:
options: Deadlock by checking if allocation leaves a safe state
questions.solution: Banker (Dijkstra): process declares max need. Grant only if safe state remains.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Interrupts are used for:
questions.solution: Interrupt = signal from hardware/software causing CPU to pause current execution.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The Y combinator:
options: Enables recursion in lambda calculus (Y = λf.(λx.f(xx))(λx.f(xx)))
questions.solution: Y combinator: Y F = F (Y F) (fixed point). Enables recursive functions in λ-calculus without self-reference.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Which statement comparing compilation and interpretation is correct?
options: Compiled code generally runs faster than interpreted code
questions.solution: Ahead-of-time compiled native code typically executes faster than line-by-line interpretation.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Virtual memory allows a program to:
options: Use more address space than physical RAM
questions.solution: Virtual memory backs part of the address space on disk, giving the illusion of large RAM.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: How many address lines are needed to address 8K memory locations?
questions.solution: 8K = 8192 = 2^13, so 13 address lines are required.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Regular languages are closed under:
options: Union, intersection, complement, concatenation, Kleene star
questions.solution: Regular languages: closed under all Boolean operations, concat, Kleene star.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Memory leak occurs when:
options: Allocated heap memory is not freed (no longer accessible)
questions.solution: Memory leak = malloc() without free() (gradually exhausts heap).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Paged segmentation combines:
questions.solution: Paged segmentation: each segment is divided into pages. Used in Intel x86 protected mode.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: SPEC CPU benchmarks:
options: A suite of CPU-intensive performance benchmarks (integer and floating-point)
questions.solution: SPEC CPU 2017: SPECspeed (throughput) and SPECrate (throughput for multiprogrammed workloads).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Leftmost derivation always replaces:
options: The leftmost non-terminal at each step
questions.solution: Leftmost derivation: replace leftmost NT. Rightmost = replace rightmost. Parse trees = equivalence.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Encapsulation in object-oriented programming refers to:
options: Bundling data with the methods that operate on it
questions.solution: Encapsulation groups state and behaviour and restricts direct access to internals.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: RAID 5 uses:
questions.solution: RAID 0 = striping. RAID 1 = mirroring. RAID 5 = striping + distributed parity. RAID 6 = dual parity.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The register that holds the current instruction being executed is:
questions.solution: IR stores the fetched instruction during execution.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A context-free grammar G = (V, Σ, P, S) consists of:
questions.solution: CFG: V = non-terminals, Σ = terminals, P = production rules, S = start symbol.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A compiler differs from an interpreter in that it:
options: Executes line by line at run time
Translates the whole program before execution
questions.solution: A compiler produces object/machine code ahead of time; an interpreter executes statements directly.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The /proc filesystem:
options: Virtual filesystem exposing kernel data structures (process info, system info)
questions.solution: /proc: pseudo-filesystem. /proc/cpuinfo, /proc/meminfo, /proc/[pid]/status. No disk storage.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Non-uniform memory access (NUMA) means:
options: Access time depends on which processor accesses which memory region
questions.solution: NUMA: each CPU has local memory (fast) and remote memory (slower).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A grammar with production S → aSb | ε generates:
questions.solution: S → aSb | ε generates a^n b^n (balanced pairs).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Virtual functions in C++ enable:
questions.solution: Virtual = late binding via vtable (runtime polymorphism).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: TLB access = 20 ns, memory access = 200 ns, TLB hit ratio = 0.95 (paged memory). The effective access time is about:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Branch target buffer (BTB):
options: Caches branch target addresses (speeds up branch resolution)
questions.solution: BTB: maps branch PC → target PC. Combined with branch predictor for 2-cycle branches.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The Pumping Lemma is used to prove:
questions.solution: Pumping lemma: if regular, strings can be 'pumped'. Contrapositive: if can't pump, not regular.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The sizeof operator in C returns:
options: The size of a type or variable in bytes (compile-time)
The runtime size of allocated memory
questions.solution: sizeof: compile-time operator. sizeof(array) / sizeof(array[0]) = element count.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Memory access = 100 ns, page-fault rate = 0.001, fault service = 10 ms. The effective access time is about:
questions.solution: EAT = (1−p)·ma + p·service = 1×100 + 0.001×10ms ≈ 10099.9 ns (faults dominate).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The CPU register that holds the address of the next instruction is:
questions.solution: PC = address of next instruction to fetch.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A derivation replaces:
options: A non-terminal with the RHS of a production rule
A variable with a terminal only
questions.solution: Derivation: S ⇒ α₁ ⇒ α₂ ⇒ ... ⇒ w (string of terminals).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Python uses ____ for indentation:
questions.solution: Python: indentation defines block structure (PEP 8 recommends 4 spaces).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The 'ls' command in Unix lists:
questions.solution: ls = list directory. ls -l = long format, ls -a = show hidden files.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: MIMD stands for:
questions.solution: MIMD: multiple processors each executing their own instructions on their own data.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The Myhill-Nerode theorem characterizes:
options: Regular languages (by number of equivalence classes)
questions.solution: Myhill-Nerode: language is regular iff the Myhill-Nerode relation has finite index.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Java's garbage collector:
options: Automatically reclaims memory of unreferenced objects
questions.solution: GC in Java: automatic heap memory management (mark-sweep, generational).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A semaphore with initial value 1 is used for:
questions.solution: Binary semaphore = mutex (init 1, wait = lock, signal = unlock).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Write-through cache writes to:
options: Both cache and main memory simultaneously
questions.solution: Write-through: consistent but slow (each write goes to RAM). Write-back: only on eviction.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Two regular expressions are equivalent if:
questions.solution: Regular expression equivalence: same set of strings described.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A virtual destructor:
options: Ensures derived class destructors are called when deleting through a base pointer
Is the same as a regular destructor
questions.solution: Virtual destructor: needed when deleting derived objects through base pointers.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Virtual memory allows:
options: Executing programs larger than physical RAM (using disk as extension)
questions.solution: Virtual memory: paging/segmentation. Pages swapped between RAM and disk (swap).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Pessimistic locking:
options: Locks resources when read (prevents other transactions from modifying)
questions.solution: Pessimistic: SELECT ... FOR UPDATE. Ensures exclusive access. Better for high contention.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A design pattern is:
options: A reusable solution to a common software design problem
questions.solution: Design patterns: Creational (Factory, Singleton), Structural (Adapter, Decorator), Behavioral (Observer, Strategy).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: SDN (Software-Defined Networking):
options: Separates control plane from data plane (centralized controller)
questions.solution: SDN: OpenFlow protocol. Controller (e.g., ONOS, OpenDaylight) programs switch forwarding tables.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A JK flip-flop eliminates the invalid state of SR by:
questions.solution: JK FF: J=K=1 → toggle. J=1,K=0 → set. J=0,K=1 → reset. J=K=0 → hold.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The B+ tree index is commonly used because:
options: It uses less memory than hash
It supports both equality and range queries efficiently
questions.solution: B+ tree: ordered structure, supports =, <, >, BETWEEN, etc.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The number of comparisons in selection sort for n=16 is:
questions.solution: Selection sort: 16*(16-1)/2 = 120 comparisons (always).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Namespaces in Linux:
options: Isolate and virtualize system resources (PID, network, mount, user, etc.)
questions.solution: Namespaces: 8 types (PID, net, mnt, uts, ipc, user, cgroup, time). Foundation of containers.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The Edmonds-Karp algorithm is:
questions.solution: Edmonds-Karp = FF with BFS for shortest augmenting path.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: HTTP status code 500 means:
questions.solution: 500 = server error. 502 = Bad Gateway. 503 = Service Unavailable. 504 = Gateway Timeout.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A 5-stage RISC pipeline has:
questions.solution: Classic RISC pipeline: IF → ID → EX → MEM → WB (5 stages).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A database trigger is:
options: A stored procedure that automatically executes on table events (INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE)
questions.solution: Trigger = reactive code that runs before/after DML operations.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A 2-3 tree allows:
options: Nodes with 1 or 2 keys (2 or 3 children)
questions.solution: 2-3 tree: 2-node (1 key, 2 children) or 3-node (2 keys, 3 children). B-tree variant.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: NaN in IEEE 754:
questions.solution: NaN: quiet NaN (QNaN) vs signaling NaN (SNaN). QNaN propagates. SNaN triggers exception.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Cache access = 20 ns, memory access = 100 ns, hit ratio = 0.95. The effective access time is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The control unit generates:
options: Control signals (read/write, ALU op, etc.) based on instructions
questions.solution: Control unit = decodes instructions and generates control signals for datapath.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The CPU datapath consists of:
options: ALU, registers, buses (paths for data flow)
questions.solution: Datapath: performs data operations (arithmetic, logic, moves).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Pipeline hazards include:
questions.solution: Structural (resource conflict), data (dependencies), control (branches).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: SMT (Simultaneous Multi-Threading) allows:
options: Multiple cores to run one thread
Multiple threads to execute on one core simultaneously
questions.solution: SMT = Intel Hyper-Threading: one core appears as multiple logical processors.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: MOESI cache protocol adds:
options: Owned state (dirty but shared; no need to write back for other sharers)
questions.solution: MOESI: O=owned (dirty, shared). Core with O state supplies data to other caches without memory access.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The worst-case time complexity of insertion sort is:
questions.solution: Insertion sort worst: O(n²) (reverse sorted). Best: O(n) (sorted).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The page size in x86 architecture is typically:
questions.solution: x86 standard page = 4 KB. Huge pages = 2 MB/1 GB.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A binomial heap supports merge in:
questions.solution: Binomial heap: merge O(log n). Fibonacci heap: merge O(1).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Quicksort attains its O(n²) worst case when:
options: The pivot is consistently the smallest or largest element
The array is large but random
The pivot is always the median
questions.solution: Maximally unbalanced partitions (e.g. sorted input with first/last pivot) give O(n²).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The minimum number of edges in a connected graph with n vertices is:
questions.solution: Tree = n-1 edges. Complete graph = n(n-1)/2 edges.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Operations on an empty stack: push 5, push 8, push 20, pop, push 30. The element now on top is:
questions.solution: After push 20 then pop (removes 20), pushing 30 leaves 30 on top (LIFO).

figure.caption: Stack state

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Kanban focuses on:
options: Visualizing workflow, limiting work in progress (WIP), and continuous delivery
questions.solution: Kanban: continuous flow (no sprints). WIP limits prevent overloading. Pull-based system.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Operations on an empty stack: push 5, push 8, push 14, pop, push 25. The element now on top is:
questions.solution: After push 14 then pop (removes 14), pushing 25 leaves 25 on top (LIFO).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Cache access = 10 ns, memory access = 100 ns, hit ratio = 0.9. The effective access time is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The binary representation of decimal 31 is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Checkpoint in database recovery:
options: Flushes dirty pages and records LSN in log (reduces recovery time after crash)
questions.solution: Checkpoint: periodic consistency point. Fuzzy checkpoint doesn't stop transactions.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Deadlock can be prevented by:
options: Ensuring at least one of the 4 Coffman conditions doesn't hold
questions.solution: Deadlock prevention: violate one of mutex, hold-and-wait, no-preemption, or circular wait.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Demand paging brings a page into memory:
options: Only when it is first referenced (causing a page fault)
At program load time, all at once
questions.solution: Pages are loaded lazily on first access; the page fault triggers the load.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Operations on an empty stack: push 7, push 8, push 14, pop, push 21. The element now on top is:
questions.solution: After push 14 then pop (removes 14), pushing 21 leaves 21 on top (LIFO).

figure.markup: <svg viewBox="0 0 220 130" width="220" height="130"><rect x="80" y="20" width="60" height="24" fill="#fee2e2" stroke="#dc2626"/><text x="110" y="37" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">21</text><rect x="80" y="44" width="60" height="24" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="110" y="61" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">8</text><rect x="80" y="68" width="60" height="24" fill="#dbeafe" stroke="#2563eb"/><text x="110" y="85" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">7</text><line x1="78" y1="20" x2="78" y2="92" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="142" y1="20" x2="142" y2="92" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="2"/><line x1="78" y1="92" x2="142" y2="92" stroke="#475569" stroke-width="2"/><text x="150" y="30" font-size="10" fill="#dc2626">← top</text></svg>
figure.caption: Stack state

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A ripple counter has the disadvantage of:
questions.solution: Ripple counter: each FF clocked by previous output → cumulative delay.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Code optimization can be:
options: Local (within basic block), global (within function), or inter-procedural
questions.solution: Optimization levels: local (peephole), global (loop, data-flow), inter-procedural.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: A splay tree is:
options: A self-adjusting BST that moves accessed elements to the root
questions.solution: Splay tree: recently accessed elements near root (amortized O(log n)).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: An INNER JOIN returns:
options: Rows with matching values in both tables
questions.solution: INNER JOIN: intersection of both tables on join condition.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The setjmp/longjmp mechanism:
options: Provides non-local gotos (jump across function calls, similar to exceptions)
questions.solution: setjmp(jmp_buf): saves stack context. longjmp(jmp_buf, val): restores context (jumps back to setjmp).

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: The Boolean expression A + A'B simplifies to:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Manacher's algorithm:
options: Finds all palindromic substrings in O(n) time
questions.solution: Manacher: linear time using palindrome radii and symmetry to avoid recomputation.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: For the AND gate shown with inputs A = 1 and B = 1, the output is:

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: Modular exponentiation (a^b mod m) can be computed in:
options: O(log b) using exponentiation by squaring
questions.solution: Exponentiation by squaring: decompose b in binary → O(log b) multiplications.

questions.subject: Paper-II · Professional Knowledge
questions.stem: SIMD extensions include:
questions.solution: SIMD: single instruction operates on multiple data elements (packed in wide registers).
